   289→risk announcing "done" with failing tests, unpushed commits, or scope creep —
   290→all of which have caused regressions in this project.
   291→
   292→### Worktree isolation for teammates
   293→
   294→When spawned as a teammate with `isolation: "worktree"`, you run in a separate
   295→copy of the repo. **Violating isolation corrupts other agents' work.**
   296→
   297→**Rules:**
   298→- **Never `cd` to the main repo**. Stay in your worktree directory (under
   299→  `.claude/worktrees/`). If unsure, run `git rev-parse --show-toplevel`.
   300→- **Never `git checkout`** to switch branches. You are already on your branch.
   301→- **Never run git commands with `-C` pointing to the main repo**. All git
   302→  operations must target your worktree.
   303→- **Verify isolation** before critical operations: `git rev-parse --show-toplevel`
   304→  must return a path containing `.claude/worktrees/`.
   305→- **Commit and push only your branch**. The team lead merges to main.
   306→
   307→**Why this matters:** All worktrees share the same `.git` directory. Running
   308→`git checkout` in the main repo changes HEAD for the main working directory,
   309→which other agents and the team lead depend on. This causes branch switching
   310→races, lost commits, and merge failures.
   311→
   312→### Worktree workflow (single-agent)
   313→
   314→When working in a git worktree (e.g. started with `--worktree`), **always merge
   315→the branch back into `main` before ending the session.** Do not leave the
   316→worktree branch unmerged without asking.
   317→
   318→**Before merging a worktree branch:**
   319→- Preview conflicts first: `git merge --no-commit --no-ff <branch>` then `git merge --abort`
   320→- Rebase long-lived worktrees onto main before merging: `git rebase main` (from the worktree)
   321→
   322→**Worktree cleanup — do NOT do it manually:**
   323→Claude Code manages worktree lifecycle automatically when invoked with
   324→`--worktree`. **Never** run `git worktree remove` or delete the worktree
   325→directory yourself — the user is prompted to keep or remove it when the session
   326→ends. Manually removing the worktree mid-session can break the working directory
   327→and cause confusing errors.
   328→
   329→<!-- BEGIN BEADS INTEGRATION -->
   330→## Issue Tracking with bd (beads)
   331→
   332→**IMPORTANT**: This project uses **bd (beads)** for ALL issue tracking. Do NOT use markdown TODOs, task lists, or other tracking methods.
   333→
   334→### Why bd?
   335→
   336→- Dependency-aware: Track blockers and relationships between issues
   337→- Git-friendly: Auto-syncs to JSONL for version control
   338→- Agent-optimized: JSON output, ready work detection, discovered-from links
   339→- Prevents duplicate tracking systems and confusion
   340→
   341→### Quick Start
   342→
   343→**Check for ready work:**
   344→
   345→```bash
   346→bd ready --json
   347→```
   348→
   349→**Create new issues:**
   350→
   351→```bash
   352→bd create "Issue title" --description="Detailed context" -t feature -p 0-4 --json  # ALWAYS specify -t
   353→bd create "Issue title" --description="What this issue is about" -t bug -p 1 --deps discovered-from:bd-123 --json
   354→```
   355→
   356→**Claim and update:**
   357→
   358→```bash
   359→bd update bd-42 --status in_progress --json
   360→bd update bd-42 --priority 1 --json
   361→```
   362→
   363→**Complete work:**
   364→
   365→```bash
   366→bd close bd-42 --reason "Completed" --json
   367→```
   368→
   369→### Issue Types
   370→
   371→- `bug` - Something broken
   372→- `feature` - New functionality
   373→- `task` - Work item (tests, docs, refactoring)
   374→- `epic` - Large feature with subtasks
   375→- `chore` - Maintenance (dependencies, tooling)
   376→
   377→### Priorities
   378→
   379→- `0` - Critical (security, data loss, broken builds)
   380→- `1` - High (major features, important bugs)
   381→- `2` - Medium (default, nice-to-have)
   382→- `3` - Low (polish, optimization)
   383→- `4` - Backlog (future ideas)
   384→
   385→### Workflow for AI Agents
   386→
   387→1. **Check ready work**: `bd ready` shows unblocked issues
   388→2. **Claim your task**: `bd update <id> --status in_progress`
   389→3. **Work on it**: Implement, test, document
   390→4. **Discover new work?** Create linked issue:
   391→   - `bd create "Found bug" --description="Details about what was found" -p 1 --deps discovered-from:<parent-id>`
   392→5. **Complete**: `bd close <id> --reason "Done"`
   393→
   394→### Auto-Sync
   395→
   396→bd automatically syncs with git:
   397→
   398→- Exports to `.beads/issues.jsonl` after changes (5s debounce)
   399→- Imports from JSONL when newer (e.g., after `git pull`)
   400→- No manual export/import needed!
   401→
   402→### Important Rules
   403→
   404→- ✅ Use bd for ALL task tracking
   405→- ✅ Always use `--json` flag for programmatic use
   406→- ✅ Link discovered work with `discovered-from` dependencies
   407→- ✅ Check `bd ready` before asking "what should I work on?"
   408→- ❌ Do NOT create markdown TODO lists
   409→- ❌ Do NOT use external issue trackers
   410→- ❌ Do NOT duplicate tracking systems
   411→
   412→For more details, see README.md and docs/QUICKSTART.md.
   413→

# Agent Instructions

This project uses **bd** (beads) for issue tracking. Run `bd onboard` to get started.

## Quick Reference

```bash
bd ready              # Find available work
bd show <id>          # View issue details
bd update <id> --status in_progress  # Claim work
bd close <id>         # Complete work
bd sync               # Sync with git
```

## Issue Title Guidelines

Titles appear in `bd list`, `bd ready`, and terminal window titles where space
is limited. The **first ~20 characters** must be meaningfully descriptive:

| Bad | Good |
|---|---|
| `Add support for symbolic integers` | `Symbolic int support` |
| `Implement the protocol message for branch data` | `Branch data protocol msg` |
| `Fix bug where path constraints are dropped` | `Path constraints dropped` |
| `Create new frontend handler for Go` | `Go frontend handler` |

- **Lead with the noun or area** ("Protocol …", "Solver …", "CLI …"), not filler verbs
- **Under 50 characters** — put detail in `--description`
- Avoid prefixes like "Add support for", "Update the", "Implement new"

## Landing the Plane (Session Completion)

**When ending a work session**, you MUST complete ALL steps below. Work is NOT complete until `git push` succeeds.

**MANDATORY WORKFLOW:**

1. **File issues for remaining work** - Create issues for anything that needs follow-up
2. **Run quality gates** (if code changed) - Tests, linters, builds
3. **Update issue status** - Close finished work, update in-progress items
4. **PUSH TO REMOTE** - This is MANDATORY:
   ```bash
   git pull --rebase
   bd sync
   git push
   git status  # MUST show "up to date with origin"
   ```
5. **Clean up** - Clear stashes, prune remote branches
6. **Verify** - All changes committed AND pushed
7. **Hand off** - Provide context for next session

**CRITICAL RULES:**
- Work is NOT complete until `git push` succeeds
- NEVER stop before pushing - that leaves work stranded locally
- NEVER say "ready to push when you are" - YOU must push
- If push fails, resolve and retry until it succeeds

## Creating Issues

### Issue Sizing: Keep Issues Small

Every issue should represent **one focused, independently completable unit of work**.
A well-sized issue can be implemented, tested, and merged in a single session.

**Target size:** 1–3 files changed, under ~200 lines of diff. If an issue looks
bigger, break it down.

**How to tell an issue is too big:**
- It touches more than one module or subsystem
- The description has multiple distinct deliverables or bullet points
- It requires changes in both core and a frontend
- You find yourself thinking "first I need to X, then Y, then Z" — each of those is its own issue
- The acceptance criteria have more than 3 items

**What to do instead:**
- Create an **epic** for the larger goal, then create small child issues under it
- Each child issue should be one logical step: "add the type", "implement the handler", "write the tests", "update the CLI"
- Use `--parent` and `--waits-for` to link children to the epic
- Use `bd dep add` for ordering constraints between children

**Examples:**

| Too big | Break into |
|---|---|
| `Add symbolic integer support` | Epic + `Int symbolic type`, `Int constraint gen`, `Int solver integration`, `Int round-trip tests` |
| `Protocol message for branch data` | `Branch data Rust types`, `Branch data TS handler`, `Branch data Go handler` |
| `Fix and test path constraint bug` | `Repro test for path constraints`, `Fix path constraint drop` |

**Rule of thumb:** If you can't describe the issue in one sentence without "and",
it's two issues.

**Always specify `--type` explicitly** — never rely on the default. Use the correct issue type:
- `feature` — delivers new user-visible or agent-visible capability (most issues are features)
- `bug` — something broken that needs fixing
- `epic` — a grouping container for related features/tasks. Not directly workable.
- `task` — narrow infrastructure chore with no user-visible effect (CI config, project init, doc-only changes). If it adds capability, it's a `feature`.

### Creating Epics

Epics must use `--waits-for-gate` so they are blocked until their children complete, and do not appear in `bd ready`:

```bash
bd create "Epic: Feature Area" -t epic -p 2 -l label1,label2 \
  -d "Description of the epic" \
  --waits-for-gate all-children
```

This makes the epic resolve automatically when all children are closed. Never leave an epic as a bare open issue with no gate — it will pollute `bd ready`.

### Creating Child Issues Under Epics

Use `--parent` to place issues under an epic, and `--waits-for` to make the epic wait for the child:

```bash
bd create "Implement feature X" -t feature -p 1 -l core,rust \
  --parent str-abc \
  --waits-for str-abc \
  -d "Description" \
  --acceptance "Acceptance criteria"
```

The `--parent` flag establishes the hierarchy. The `--waits-for str-abc` flag tells the epic to wait for this child before it can close.

### Dependencies Between Issues

Use `bd dep add` to express ordering constraints between issues:

```bash
bd dep add str-child str-dependency   # str-child is blocked by str-dependency
```

Only add dependencies where there is a real technical ordering constraint (e.g., "the executor cannot be built before the instrumentor exists"). Do not add dependencies for soft preferences or nice-to-have ordering.

### Bug Fix Policy: Reproduce Before Fixing

All bugs must be reproduced in a good automated test **before** attempting any fix.
The workflow is:

1. Write a test that demonstrates the bug (the test must **fail**)
2. Verify the test fails for the right reason
3. Implement the fix
4. Verify the test now **passes**

This ensures every bug fix is validated by a regression test and prevents
"fixes" that don't actually address the root cause.

### Completing an Issue

An issue is not complete until:
1. All code changes pass quality gates (tests, clippy/lint, build)
2. Changes are committed on a **dedicated branch for this issue only** (no other issues' changes)
3. The branch is merged to `main` (one branch at a time — do not merge other branches simultaneously)
4. The branch is deleted after merge
5. The issue is closed with `bd close <id>`
6. Changes are pushed to remote with `bd sync && git push`

Do not leave stale branches. Merge and delete promptly.

**Never** combine work for multiple issues on one branch. If you discover
additional work while on an issue branch, create a new issue and report it to
the team lead (or note it for later) — do NOT implement it yourself. Address
it on a separate branch after merging the current one. Scope creep across
agents is the primary cause of duplicate commits and orphan branches.

  function is implemented, a bug is fixed — commit it immediately.
- **Push your branch frequently.** A local commit is better than uncommitted
  changes, but a pushed commit survives everything. Push after every 1–2 commits.
- **Never hold work "until it's ready."** A series of small, incremental commits
  on a feature branch is vastly better than one big commit that might never land.
- **Teammates in worktrees**: push your branch before reporting task completion.

This links commits to the issue they address and makes history easy to trace.

Format: `<issue-key>: <description>`

```
str-a1b: Add symbolic integer support to concolic engine

Implements concrete+symbolic tracking for i32/i64 values with
path constraint generation for branch conditions.
```

When a commit addresses multiple issues, use the primary issue as the prefix
and list others in the body:
```

Implements concrete+symbolic tracking for i32/i64 values with
path constraint generation for branch conditions.

```

When a commit includes changes to beads data (`.beads/` files) without a
specific feature issue, list all affected issue IDs in the body:
```
bd sync: close str-a1b, str-c2d

Issues: str-a1b, str-c2d
```

This ensures the user can review proposed approaches before code is written.

**Quick start:** Use `/swarm` to automate the full parallel workflow — triage,

**Manual workflow (if not using `/swarm`):**

### Worktree isolation for teammates

When spawned as a teammate with `isolation: "worktree"`, you run in a separate

**Rules:**
- **Never `cd` to the main repo**. Stay in your worktree directory (under

- **Never `git checkout`** to switch branches. You are already on your branch.
- **Never run git commands with `-C` pointing to the main repo**. All git
  operations must target your worktree.
- **Verify isolation** before critical operations: `git rev-parse --show-toplevel`
  must return a path containing `.claude/worktrees/`.
- **Commit and push only your branch**. The team lead merges to main.

**Why this matters:** All worktrees share the same `.git` directory. Running
`git checkout` in the main repo changes HEAD for the main working directory,
which other agents and the team lead depend on. This causes branch switching
races, lost commits, and merge failures.

### Worktree workflow (single-agent)

When working in a git worktree (e.g. started with `--worktree`), **always merge
the branch back into `main` before ending the session.** Do not leave the
worktree branch unmerged without asking.

**Before merging a worktree branch:**
- Preview conflicts first: `git merge --no-commit --no-ff <branch>` then `git merge --abort`
- Rebase long-lived worktrees onto main before merging: `git rebase main` (from the worktree)

**Worktree cleanup — do NOT do it manually:**
Claude Code manages worktree lifecycle automatically when invoked with
`--worktree`. **Never** run `git worktree remove` or delete the worktree
directory yourself — the user is prompted to keep or remove it when the session
ends. Manually removing the worktree mid-session can break the working directory
and cause confusing errors.

## Issue Tracking with bd (beads)

**IMPORTANT**: This project uses **bd (beads)** for ALL issue tracking. Do NOT use markdown TODOs, task lists, or other tracking methods.

### Why bd?

- Dependency-aware: Track blockers and relationships between issues
- Git-friendly: Auto-syncs to JSONL for version control
- Agent-optimized: JSON output, ready work detection, discovered-from links
- Prevents duplicate tracking systems and confusion

### Quick Start

**Check for ready work:**

```bash
bd ready --json
```

**Create new issues:**

```bash
bd create "Issue title" --description="Detailed context" -t feature -p 0-4 --json  # ALWAYS specify -t
bd create "Issue title" --description="What this issue is about" -t bug -p 1 --deps discovered-from:bd-123 --json
```

**Claim and update:**

```bash
bd update bd-42 --status in_progress --json
bd update bd-42 --priority 1 --json
```

**Complete work:**

```bash
bd close bd-42 --reason "Completed" --json
```

### Issue Types

- `bug` - Something broken
- `feature` - New functionality
- `task` - Work item (tests, docs, refactoring)
- `epic` - Large feature with subtasks
- `chore` - Maintenance (dependencies, tooling)

### Priorities

- `0` - Critical (security, data loss, broken builds)
- `1` - High (major features, important bugs)
- `2` - Medium (default, nice-to-have)
- `3` - Low (polish, optimization)
- `4` - Backlog (future ideas)

### Workflow for AI Agents

1. **Check ready work**: `bd ready` shows unblocked issues
2. **Claim your task**: `bd update <id> --status in_progress`
3. **Work on it**: Implement, test, document
4. **Discover new work?** Create linked issue:
   - `bd create "Found bug" --description="Details about what was found" -p 1 --deps discovered-from:<parent-id>`
5. **Complete**: `bd close <id> --reason "Done"`

### Auto-Sync

bd automatically syncs with git:

- Exports to `.beads/issues.jsonl` after changes (5s debounce)
- Imports from JSONL when newer (e.g., after `git pull`)
- No manual export/import needed!

### Important Rules

- ✅ Use bd for ALL task tracking
- ✅ Always use `--json` flag for programmatic use
- ✅ Link discovered work with `discovered-from` dependencies
- ✅ Check `bd ready` before asking "what should I work on?"
- ❌ Do NOT create markdown TODO lists
- ❌ Do NOT use external issue trackers
- ❌ Do NOT duplicate tracking systems

For more details, see README.md and docs/QUICKSTART.md.

# Multi-Level Setup/Teardown for Shatter

## Context

Users need in-process setup and teardown for tests executed by Shatter — initializing databases, authentication keys, external services, etc. The current system supports two levels (`per_function` and `per_execution`) but lacks session-level and file-level setup, convention-based discovery, proper error propagation, separate timeouts, and implementations in Go/Rust frontends. The orchestrator path (`orchestrator.rs`) also completely ignores setup, violating the parallel-path parity rule.

No backward compatibility constraints — we can redesign the protocol cleanly.

## User-Facing Examples

### TypeScript Setup Files

**Session-level setup** (`shatter.setup.ts` in project root, or `.shatter/setup.ts`):

```typescript
// shatter.setup.ts — runs once for the entire shatter session

import { Pool } from "pg";

let pool: Pool;

export async function setup(): Promise<{ db: Pool }> {
  pool = new Pool({ connectionString: process.env.DATABASE_URL });
  await pool.query("CREATE SCHEMA IF NOT EXISTS shatter_test");
  return { db: pool };
}

export async function teardown(context: { db: Pool }): Promise<void> {
  await context.db.query("DROP SCHEMA IF EXISTS shatter_test CASCADE");
  await context.db.end();
}
```

**File-level setup** (`src/db/users.shatter.setup.ts` co-located, or configured via glob):

```typescript
// users.shatter.setup.ts — runs once per source file being explored

export async function setup(
  scope: string,             // the source file path being explored
  parentContext: { db: Pool } // context from session setup
): Promise<{ table: string }> {
  const table = `test_users_${Date.now()}`;
  await parentContext.db.query(`CREATE TABLE ${table} (id SERIAL, name TEXT)`);
  return { table };
}

export async function teardown(
  scope: string,
  context: { table: string },
  parentContext: { db: Pool }
): Promise<void> {
  await parentContext.db.query(`DROP TABLE IF EXISTS ${context.table}`);
}
```

**Function-level setup** (configured in `.shatter/config.yaml`):

```typescript
// .shatter/setup/auth-setup.ts — runs once per function

export async function setup(
  scope: string,                              // function name
  parentContext: { db: Pool; table: string }   // merged session + file contexts
): Promise<{ authToken: string }> {
  const token = await generateTestToken(parentContext.db);
  return { authToken: token };
}

export async function teardown(
  scope: string,
  context: { authToken: string },
  parentContext: { db: Pool; table: string }
): Promise<void> {
  await revokeToken(context.authToken, parentContext.db);
}
```

**Execution-level setup** (runs before every single execution of a function):

```typescript
// .shatter/setup/per-exec-setup.ts — runs before each execution

export function setup(
  scope: string,
  parentContext: { db: Pool; table: string; authToken: string }
): { requestId: string } {
  return { requestId: crypto.randomUUID() };
}

// teardown is optional — if omitted, no teardown runs at this level
```

### Go Setup Files

**Session-level setup** (`shatter_setup.go` in project root):

```go
package shatter_setup

import (
    "database/sql"
    _ "github.com/lib/pq"
)

func Setup() (map[string]any, error) {
    db, err := sql.Open("postgres", os.Getenv("DATABASE_URL"))
    if err != nil {
        return nil, err
    }
    return map[string]any{"db_dsn": os.Getenv("DATABASE_URL")}, nil
}

func Teardown(context map[string]any) error {
    // cleanup
    return nil
}
```

### Rust Setup Files

**Session-level setup** (`shatter_setup.rs` in project root):

```rust
// Compiled and executed as a subprocess that communicates via JSON on stdout

use serde_json::json;

fn main() {
    // Setup logic
    let context = json!({"db_url": std::env::var("DATABASE_URL").unwrap()});
    println!("{}", context); // return context as JSON
}
```

### Configuration

**Convention-based** (zero config — just place files):

```
my-project/
├── shatter.setup.ts              # session setup (auto-discovered)
├── src/
│   ├── db/
│   │   ├── users.ts              # source file under test
│   │   └── users.shatter.setup.ts # file-level setup (auto-discovered)
│   └── auth/
│       ├── login.ts
│       └── login.shatter.setup.ts
└── .shatter/
    └── config.yaml               # optional overrides
```

**Config-based** (`.shatter/config.yaml`):

```yaml
# Session-level setup — runs once for the entire shatter run
session_setup:
  file: ./setup/global-init.ts
  timeout: 60   # seconds

# File-level setup — glob patterns map source files to setup files
file_setup:
  "src/db/**":   ./setup/db-file-init.ts
  "src/auth/**": ./setup/auth-file-init.ts

# Function-level defaults
defaults:
  setup: ./setup/default-function-setup.ts
  setup_level: function     # function | execution
  setup_timeout: 30

# Per-function overrides
functions:
  "src/auth.ts:validateToken":
    setup: ./setup/auth-token-setup.ts
    setup_level: execution   # run before every execution
    setup_timeout: 10
```

**CLI usage:**

```bash
# Basic — convention discovery handles everything
shatter explore src/db/users.ts

# With explicit setup timeout
shatter explore src/db/users.ts --setup-timeout 60

# Abort on any setup failure
shatter explore src/db/users.ts --fail-on-error

# Scan with setup (session setup runs once, file setups per file)
shatter scan src/ --fail-on-error --setup-timeout 120
```

## Setup Levels

| Level | Scope | Setup function signature |
|-------|-------|------------------------|
| **Session** | Once for entire shatter run | `setup() → context` |
| **File** | Once per source file | `setup(filePath, sessionContext) → context` |
| **Function** | Once per function | `setup(functionName, {session, file}) → context` |
| **Execution** | Per individual execution | `setup(functionName, {session, file, function}) → context` |

Each level's `setup()` receives the accumulated parent contexts. Each level's `teardown()` receives its own context plus the parent contexts.

## Design

### 1. Protocol

Replace `SetupMode` with `SetupLevel` everywhere. Remove backward-compat shims.

**`SetupLevel` enum:**

```rust
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, Deserialize, Serialize)]
#[serde(rename_all = "snake_case")]
pub enum SetupLevel {
    Session,
    File,
    Function,
    Execution,
}
```

**`Setup` command:**

```rust
Setup {
    file: String,           // path to the setup file
    scope: String,          // "" for session, file path for file, fn name for function/execution
    level: SetupLevel,
    parent_context: Option<SetupContextStack>,  // accumulated parent contexts
    project_root: Option<String>,
}
```

**`Teardown` command:**

```rust
Teardown {
    scope: String,
    level: SetupLevel,
}
```

**`SetupContextStack`** (replaces flat `setup_context`):

```rust
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Default, Serialize, Deserialize)]
pub struct SetupContextStack {
    pub session: Option<serde_json::Value>,
    pub file: Option<serde_json::Value>,
    pub function: Option<serde_json::Value>,
    pub execution: Option<serde_json::Value>,
}
```

**`Execute` command** — `setup_context: Option<Value>` → `setup_context: Option<SetupContextStack>`

**`Setup` response** — unchanged: `Setup { setup_context: Value }`

The core engine places each level's returned context into the appropriate `SetupContextStack` slot before sending Execute commands.

### 2. Config Schema

New fields in `ShatterConfig` (top level):
- `session_setup: Option<SetupConfig>` where `SetupConfig { file: String, timeout: Option<u64> }`
- `file_setup: Option<HashMap<String, String>>` (glob → setup file path)

Rename in `DefaultsConfig` and `FunctionConfig`:
- `setup_mode: Option<SetupMode>` → `setup_level: Option<SetupLevel>`
- Add `setup_timeout: Option<u64>`

`ResolvedFunctionConfig`:
- `setup_mode: SetupMode` → `setup_level: SetupLevel`
- Add `setup_timeout: u64`

### 3. Convention-Based Discovery

New function `discover_setup_files()` in `config.rs`:

| Level | Convention | Location |
|-------|-----------|----------|
| Session | `shatter.setup.{ext}` | Project root |
| Session | `.shatter/setup.{ext}` | Inside `.shatter/` dir |
| File | `<stem>.shatter.setup.{ext}` | Co-located with source file |

Where `{ext}` matches the frontend language (`ts`/`js` for TypeScript, `go` for Go, `rs` for Rust).

Config-based setup always takes precedence over convention-discovered files.

### 4. SetupManager (new module: `shatter-core/src/setup_manager.rs`)

Centralizes all setup lifecycle logic, replacing the inline logic in `explorer.rs`:

```rust
pub struct SetupManager {
    session_context: Option<Value>,
    file_contexts: HashMap<String, Value>,
    function_contexts: HashMap<String, Value>,
    failures: SetupFailures,
    fail_on_error: bool,
    timeouts: SetupTimeouts,
}

pub struct SetupTimeouts {
    pub session: Duration,
    pub file: Duration,
    pub function: Duration,
    pub execution: Duration,
}

pub struct SetupFailures {
    pub session: Option<String>,
    pub files: HashMap<String, String>,
    pub functions: HashMap<String, String>,
}
```

Key methods:
- `setup_session()` / `teardown_session()` — called by CLI/scan orchestrator
- `setup_file()` / `teardown_file()` — called when transitioning between source files
- `setup_function()` / `teardown_function()` — called by explorer/orchestrator per function
- `setup_execution()` / `teardown_execution()` — called per execution in the explore loop
- `context_stack(file, function)` → builds `SetupContextStack` from active contexts
- `should_skip_file(file)` / `should_skip_function(fn)` → checks failures

All setup calls are wrapped with `tokio::time::timeout()` using per-level timeouts.

### 5. Error Propagation

| Failure level | Effect | `--fail-on-error` |
|---------------|--------|-------------------|
| Session | All functions across all files skipped | Abort immediately |
| File | All functions in that file skipped | Abort immediately |
| Function | That function skipped | Abort immediately |
| Execution | Recorded as error, loop continues | Abort immediately |

Setup failures are reported in the exploration output with the skip reason.

### 6. Timeout Configuration

| Level | Default | Env var override |
|-------|---------|-----------------|
| Session | 120s | `SHATTER_SETUP_TIMEOUT` (global ceiling) |
| File | 30s | `SHATTER_SETUP_TIMEOUT` |
| Function | 15s | `SHATTER_SETUP_TIMEOUT` |
| Execution | 5s | `SHATTER_SETUP_TIMEOUT` |

CLI: `--setup-timeout <seconds>` sets the global ceiling. Per-level defaults can be overridden in config (`session_setup.timeout`, `defaults.setup_timeout`, per-function `setup_timeout`).

### 7. CLI Flags

- `--setup-timeout <seconds>` — global setup/teardown timeout ceiling
- `--fail-on-error` — abort on any setup failure (default: skip dependents + continue)

## File Changes

### Phase 1: Protocol & Types

| File | Changes |
|------|---------|
| `shatter-core/src/config.rs` | Replace `SetupMode` with `SetupLevel`. Add `SessionSetupConfig`, `file_setup` field, `setup_timeout` fields, `discover_setup_files()`. Update `ResolvedFunctionConfig`. |
| `shatter-core/src/protocol.rs` | Replace `mode` with `level` in Setup/Teardown. Add `parent_context` to Setup. Change `setup_context` in Execute to `SetupContextStack`. Add `SetupContextStack` type. Rename `function` to `scope` in Setup/Teardown. |
| `shatter-core/src/test_arbitraries.rs` | Replace `arb_setup_mode()` with `arb_setup_level()`. Add `arb_setup_context_stack()`. |
| `shatter-ts/src/protocol.ts` | Replace `SetupMode` with `SetupLevel`. Update request/response interfaces. |
| `shatter-go/protocol/types.go` | Mirror protocol changes. |
| `shatter-rust/src/protocol.rs` | Mirror protocol changes. |

### Phase 2: SetupManager

| File | Changes |
|------|---------|
| `shatter-core/src/setup_manager.rs` | **New** — all setup lifecycle, context tracking, failure tracking, timeout |
| `shatter-core/src/lib.rs` | Add `pub mod setup_manager;` |

### Phase 3: Explorer & Orchestrator Integration

| File | Changes |
|------|---------|
| `shatter-core/src/explorer.rs` | Remove `send_setup`/`send_teardown` functions and inline lifecycle. Replace `setup_file`/`setup_mode` in `ExploreConfig` with `SetupManager` reference. Use `context_stack()` for Execute commands. |
| `shatter-core/src/orchestrator.rs` | Add setup support to `ExploreConfig`. Use `SetupManager` for function/execution setup. Replace `setup_context: None` with `context_stack()`. |
| `shatter-core/src/scan_orchestrator.rs` | Wire session + file setup lifecycle. `SetupManager::setup_session` before scan, `setup_file` per file, teardowns in reverse. |

### Phase 4: CLI Wiring

| File | Changes |
|------|---------|
| `shatter-cli/src/main.rs` | Add `--setup-timeout` and `--fail-on-error` flags. Resolve setup files from config + convention. Set `SHATTER_SETUP_TIMEOUT` env var. Wire setup config into all explore/scan paths. |

### Phase 5: Frontend Implementations

**TypeScript (extend existing):**

| File | Changes |
|------|---------|
| `shatter-ts/src/handlers.ts` | Handle `level` field and `parent_context`. Separate context caches per level. Pass parent contexts to setup functions. |
| `shatter-ts/src/setup-loader.ts` | Update `runSetup()` to accept `SetupLevel` and `parentContext`. Setup function signature: `setup(scope, parentContext?)`. Support async setup/teardown. |
| `shatter-ts/src/executor.ts` | Read `SHATTER_SETUP_TIMEOUT` for setup-specific timeout. |

**Go (implement from stubs):**

| File | Changes |
|------|---------|
| `shatter-go/protocol/handler.go` | Replace stubs with real implementations. Load Go setup files as compiled plugins or subprocess. Cache contexts per level. |
| `shatter-go/setup/loader.go` | **New** — Go setup module loader. |

**Rust (implement from stubs):**

| File | Changes |
|------|---------|
| `shatter-rust/src/handler.rs` | Replace stubs. Rust setup files compiled and executed as subprocesses, returning JSON context on stdout. |
| `shatter-rust/src/setup.rs` | **New** — Rust setup execution logic. |

### Phase 6: Convention Discovery

| File | Changes |
|------|---------|
| `shatter-core/src/config.rs` | `discover_setup_files()` — filesystem check for convention patterns |

## Testing

1. **Protocol round-trips** — all new types in all 4 language implementations
2. **SetupManager unit tests** — lifecycle ordering, context merging, failure propagation, timeouts
3. **SetupManager proptest** — arbitrary setup/teardown sequences maintain valid state
4. **Config tests** — convention discovery, config merging with new fields, `SetupLevel` serialization
5. **E2E tests** — new cases in `e2e_concolic.rs` exercising session + file setup with TS frontend
6. **Parity tests** — explorer.rs and orchestrator.rs produce identical behavior with setup
7. **Walkthrough** — add setup/teardown demo to `demo/walkthrough.sh`
8. **Frontend tests** — handler tests for each level in TS, Go, Rust

## Verification

1. `cargo test` — all core tests pass (Quick tier)
2. `cargo clippy -- -D warnings` — clean (Standard tier)
3. `cd shatter-ts && npm test` — TS handler/setup tests pass
4. `cd shatter-go && go test ./...` — Go handler/setup tests pass
5. `cd shatter-rust && cargo test` — Rust handler/setup tests pass
6. `cargo test --test e2e_concolic` — E2E pipeline with setup still works
7. `bash demo/walkthrough.sh --auto --delay 0` — walkthrough passes

## Implementation Order

Phases 1→6 are sequential. Phase 5 (TS/Go/Rust frontends) can be parallelized via separate worktrees. Each phase must pass its test tier before proceeding.

# Multi-Level Setup/Teardown — Issue Breakdown

## Context

Users need in-process setup and teardown for tests executed by Shatter — initializing databases, authentication keys, external services, etc. The current system supports two levels (`per_function` and `per_execution`) but lacks session-level and file-level setup, convention-based discovery, proper error propagation, separate timeouts, and implementations in Go/Rust frontends. The orchestrator path (`orchestrator.rs`) also completely ignores setup, violating the parallel-path parity rule.

No backward compatibility constraints — we can redesign the protocol cleanly.

Design reference: see the full implementation plan in the user's message for protocol types, config schema, SetupManager design, error propagation rules, timeout defaults, CLI flags, convention patterns, and user-facing examples.

---

## Epic

**Title:** Multi-level setup/teardown
**Type:** epic
**Priority:** 1
**Labels:** core, protocol, setup
**Description:** Four-level setup/teardown lifecycle (session → file → function → execution) with convention-based discovery, per-level timeouts, error propagation, and implementations in all frontends. Replaces the current two-level SetupMode system.
**Gate:** `--waits-for-gate all-children`

---

## Issues

### 1. SetupLevel protocol types

**Type:** feature | **Priority:** 1 | **Labels:** core, protocol
**Parent:** epic
**Files:** `shatter-core/src/protocol.rs`, `shatter-core/src/test_arbitraries.rs`
**Description:**
Replace `SetupMode` with `SetupLevel` enum (session/file/function/execution). Add `SetupContextStack` type. Update Setup command: rename `function` → `scope`, replace `mode` → `level`, add `parent_context: Option<SetupContextStack>`. Update Teardown: rename `function` → `scope`, replace `mode` → `level`. Change Execute `setup_context` from `Option<Value>` to `Option<SetupContextStack>`. Update test_arbitraries: replace `arb_setup_mode()` with `arb_setup_level()`, add `arb_setup_context_stack()`. Include proptest roundtrips for all new types.
**Acceptance:** `cargo test -p shatter-core` passes, `cargo clippy -p shatter-core -- -D warnings` clean.
**Blocked by:** nothing

### 2. Config schema for multi-level setup

**Type:** feature | **Priority:** 1 | **Labels:** core, config
**Parent:** epic
**Files:** `shatter-core/src/config.rs`
**Description:**
Replace `SetupMode` with `SetupLevel` throughout config types. Add `SessionSetupConfig { file: String, timeout: Option<u64> }`. Add `session_setup: Option<SessionSetupConfig>` and `file_setup: Option<HashMap<String, String>>` (glob → setup file path) to `ShatterConfig`. Add `setup_timeout: Option<u64>` to `DefaultsConfig` and `FunctionConfig`. Update `ResolvedFunctionConfig`: rename `setup_mode` → `setup_level`, add `setup_timeout: u64`. Add config deserialization tests for new fields.
**Acceptance:** Config round-trips with new fields. `cargo test -p shatter-core` passes.
**Blocked by:** #1 (uses SetupLevel type)

### 3. Convention-based setup discovery

**Type:** feature | **Priority:** 2 | **Labels:** core, config
**Parent:** epic
**Files:** `shatter-core/src/config.rs`
**Description:**
Add `discover_setup_files(project_root, language_ext)` function. Conventions: session-level checks `shatter.setup.{ext}` in project root and `.shatter/setup.{ext}`; file-level checks `<stem>.shatter.setup.{ext}` co-located with source file. Config-based setup takes precedence over convention-discovered files. Unit tests with temp directories verifying discovery for each level and each language extension.
**Acceptance:** Discovery finds convention files, config overrides convention, tests pass.
**Blocked by:** #2 (integrates with config resolution)

### 4. SetupManager module

**Type:** feature | **Priority:** 1 | **Labels:** core, setup
**Parent:** epic
**Files:** `shatter-core/src/setup_manager.rs` (new), `shatter-core/src/lib.rs`
**Description:**
New module centralizing setup lifecycle. `SetupManager` struct with: session/file/function context caches, `SetupFailures` tracker, `SetupTimeouts` with per-level defaults (120s/30s/15s/5s), `fail_on_error` flag. Methods: `setup_session()`/`teardown_session()`, `setup_file()`/`teardown_file()`, `setup_function()`/`teardown_function()`, `setup_execution()`/`teardown_execution()`, `context_stack(file, fn)` → `SetupContextStack`, `should_skip_file()`/`should_skip_function()`. All setup calls wrapped with `tokio::time::timeout()`. Supports `SHATTER_SETUP_TIMEOUT` env var as global ceiling. Unit tests for lifecycle ordering, context merging, failure propagation, timeout behavior. Proptest for arbitrary setup/teardown sequences maintaining valid state.
**Acceptance:** `cargo test -p shatter-core` passes including SetupManager unit + proptest. Clippy clean.
**Blocked by:** #1 (uses SetupLevel, SetupContextStack), #2 (uses config types)

### 5. Explorer SetupManager integration

**Type:** feature | **Priority:** 1 | **Labels:** core, explorer
**Parent:** epic

**Description:**
Remove `send_setup`/`send_teardown` helper functions and inline setup lifecycle from explorer. Replace `setup_file`/`setup_mode` in `ExploreConfig` with `SetupManager` reference. Teardowns run in reverse order. If session setup fails and `fail_on_error` is set, abort scan immediately. Otherwise skip all functions and continue to next file.

# Multi-Level Setup/Teardown for Shatter

## Context

Users need in-process setup and teardown for tests executed by Shatter — initializing databases, authentication keys, external services, etc. The current system supports two levels (`per_function` and `per_execution`) but lacks session-level and file-level setup, convention-based discovery, proper error propagation, separate timeouts, and implementations in Go/Rust frontends. The orchestrator path (`orchestrator.rs`) also completely ignores setup, violating the parallel-path parity rule.

No backward compatibility constraints — we can redesign the protocol cleanly.

## User-Facing Examples

### TypeScript Setup Files

**Session-level setup** (`shatter.setup.ts` in project root, or `.shatter/setup.ts`):

```typescript
// shatter.setup.ts — runs once for the entire shatter session

import { Pool } from "pg";

let pool: Pool;

export async function setup(): Promise<{ db: Pool }> {
  pool = new Pool({ connectionString: process.env.DATABASE_URL });
  await pool.query("CREATE SCHEMA IF NOT EXISTS shatter_test");
  return { db: pool };
}

export async function teardown(context: { db: Pool }): Promise<void> {
  await context.db.query("DROP SCHEMA IF EXISTS shatter_test CASCADE");
  await context.db.end();
}
```

**File-level setup** (`src/db/users.shatter.setup.ts` co-located, or configured via glob):

```typescript
// users.shatter.setup.ts — runs once per source file being explored

export async function setup(
  scope: string,             // the source file path being explored
  parentContext: { db: Pool } // context from session setup
): Promise<{ table: string }> {
  const table = `test_users_${Date.now()}`;
  await parentContext.db.query(`CREATE TABLE ${table} (id SERIAL, name TEXT)`);
  return { table };
}

export async function teardown(
  scope: string,
  context: { table: string },
  parentContext: { db: Pool }
): Promise<void> {
  await parentContext.db.query(`DROP TABLE IF EXISTS ${context.table}`);
}
```

**Function-level setup** (configured in `.shatter/config.yaml`):

```typescript
// .shatter/setup/auth-setup.ts — runs once per function

export async function setup(
  scope: string,                              // function name
  parentContext: { db: Pool; table: string }   // merged session + file contexts
): Promise<{ authToken: string }> {
  const token = await generateTestToken(parentContext.db);
  return { authToken: token };
}

export async function teardown(
  scope: string,
  context: { authToken: string },
  parentContext: { db: Pool; table: string }
): Promise<void> {
  await revokeToken(context.authToken, parentContext.db);
}
```

**Execution-level setup** (runs before every single execution of a function):

```typescript
// .shatter/setup/per-exec-setup.ts — runs before each execution

export function setup(
  scope: string,
  parentContext: { db: Pool; table: string; authToken: string }
): { requestId: string } {
  return { requestId: crypto.randomUUID() };
}

// teardown is optional — if omitted, no teardown runs at this level
```

### Go Setup Files

**Session-level setup** (`shatter_setup.go` in project root):

```go
package shatter_setup

import (
    "database/sql"
    _ "github.com/lib/pq"
)

func Setup() (map[string]any, error) {
    db, err := sql.Open("postgres", os.Getenv("DATABASE_URL"))
    if err != nil {
        return nil, err
    }
    return map[string]any{"db_dsn": os.Getenv("DATABASE_URL")}, nil
}

func Teardown(context map[string]any) error {
    // cleanup
    return nil
}
```

### Rust Setup Files

**Session-level setup** (`shatter_setup.rs` in project root):

```rust
// Compiled and executed as a subprocess that communicates via JSON on stdout

use serde_json::json;

fn main() {
    // Setup logic
    let context = json!({"db_url": std::env::var("DATABASE_URL").unwrap()});
    println!("{}", context); // return context as JSON
}
```

### Configuration

**Convention-based** (zero config — just place files):

```
my-project/
├── shatter.setup.ts              # session setup (auto-discovered)
├── src/
│   ├── db/
│   │   ├── users.ts              # source file under test
│   │   └── users.shatter.setup.ts # file-level setup (auto-discovered)
│   └── auth/
│       ├── login.ts
│       └── login.shatter.setup.ts
└── .shatter/
    └── config.yaml               # optional overrides
```

**Config-based** (`.shatter/config.yaml`):

```yaml
# Session-level setup — runs once for the entire shatter run
session_setup:
  file: ./setup/global-init.ts
  timeout: 60   # seconds

# File-level setup — glob patterns map source files to setup files
file_setup:
  "src/db/**":   ./setup/db-file-init.ts
  "src/auth/**": ./setup/auth-file-init.ts

# Function-level defaults
defaults:
  setup: ./setup/default-function-setup.ts
  setup_level: function     # function | execution
  setup_timeout: 30

# Per-function overrides
functions:
  "src/auth.ts:validateToken":
    setup: ./setup/auth-token-setup.ts
    setup_level: execution   # run before every execution
    setup_timeout: 10
```

**CLI usage:**

```bash
# Basic — convention discovery handles everything
shatter explore src/db/users.ts

# With explicit setup timeout
shatter explore src/db/users.ts --setup-timeout 60

# Abort on any setup failure
shatter explore src/db/users.ts --fail-on-error

# Scan with setup (session setup runs once, file setups per file)
shatter scan src/ --fail-on-error --setup-timeout 120
```

## Setup Levels

| Level | Scope | Setup function signature |
|-------|-------|------------------------|
| **Session** | Once for entire shatter run | `setup() → context` |
| **File** | Once per source file | `setup(filePath, sessionContext) → context` |
| **Function** | Once per function | `setup(functionName, {session, file}) → context` |
| **Execution** | Per individual execution | `setup(functionName, {session, file, function}) → context` |

Each level's `setup()` receives the accumulated parent contexts. Each level's `teardown()` receives its own context plus the parent contexts.

## Design

### 1. Protocol

Replace `SetupMode` with `SetupLevel` everywhere. Remove backward-compat shims.

**`SetupLevel` enum:**

```rust
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, Deserialize, Serialize)]
#[serde(rename_all = "snake_case")]
pub enum SetupLevel {
    Session,
    File,
    Function,
    Execution,
}
```

**`Setup` command:**

```rust
Setup {
    file: String,           // path to the setup file
    scope: String,          // "" for session, file path for file, fn name for function/execution
    level: SetupLevel,
    parent_context: Option<SetupContextStack>,  // accumulated parent contexts
    project_root: Option<String>,
}
```

**`Teardown` command:**

```rust
Teardown {
    scope: String,
    level: SetupLevel,
}
```

**`SetupContextStack`** (replaces flat `setup_context`):

```rust
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Default, Serialize, Deserialize)]
pub struct SetupContextStack {
    pub session: Option<serde_json::Value>,
    pub file: Option<serde_json::Value>,
    pub function: Option<serde_json::Value>,
    pub execution: Option<serde_json::Value>,
}
```

**`Execute` command** — `setup_context: Option<Value>` → `setup_context: Option<SetupContextStack>`

**`Setup` response** — unchanged: `Setup { setup_context: Value }`

The core engine places each level's returned context into the appropriate `SetupContextStack` slot before sending Execute commands.

### 2. Config Schema

New fields in `ShatterConfig` (top level):
- `session_setup: Option<SetupConfig>` where `SetupConfig { file: String, timeout: Option<u64> }`
- `file_setup: Option<HashMap<String, String>>` (glob → setup file path)

Rename in `DefaultsConfig` and `FunctionConfig`:
- `setup_mode: Option<SetupMode>` → `setup_level: Option<SetupLevel>`
- Add `setup_timeout: Option<u64>`

`ResolvedFunctionConfig`:
- `setup_mode: SetupMode` → `setup_level: SetupLevel`
- Add `setup_timeout: u64`

### 3. Convention-Based Discovery

New function `discover_setup_files()` in `config.rs`:

| Level | Convention | Location |
|-------|-----------|----------|
| Session | `shatter.setup.{ext}` | Project root |
| Session | `.shatter/setup.{ext}` | Inside `.shatter/` dir |
| File | `<stem>.shatter.setup.{ext}` | Co-located with source file |

Where `{ext}` matches the frontend language (`ts`/`js` for TypeScript, `go` for Go, `rs` for Rust).

Config-based setup always takes precedence over convention-discovered files.

### 4. SetupManager (new module: `shatter-core/src/setup_manager.rs`)

Centralizes all setup lifecycle logic, replacing the inline logic in `explorer.rs`:

```rust
pub struct SetupManager {
    session_context: Option<Value>,
    file_contexts: HashMap<String, Value>,
    function_contexts: HashMap<String, Value>,
    failures: SetupFailures,
    fail_on_error: bool,
    timeouts: SetupTimeouts,
}

pub struct SetupTimeouts {
    pub session: Duration,
    pub file: Duration,
    pub function: Duration,
    pub execution: Duration,
}

pub struct SetupFailures {
    pub session: Option<String>,
    pub files: HashMap<String, String>,
    pub functions: HashMap<String, String>,
}
```

Key methods:
- `setup_session()` / `teardown_session()` — called by CLI/scan orchestrator
- `setup_file()` / `teardown_file()` — called when transitioning between source files
- `setup_function()` / `teardown_function()` — called by explorer/orchestrator per function
- `setup_execution()` / `teardown_execution()` — called per execution in the explore loop
- `context_stack(file, function)` → builds `SetupContextStack` from active contexts
- `should_skip_file(file)` / `should_skip_function(fn)` → checks failures

All setup calls are wrapped with `tokio::time::timeout()` using per-level timeouts.

### 5. Error Propagation

| Failure level | Effect | `--fail-on-error` |
|---------------|--------|-------------------|
| Session | All functions across all files skipped | Abort immediately |
| File | All functions in that file skipped | Abort immediately |
| Function | That function skipped | Abort immediately |
| Execution | Recorded as error, loop continues | Abort immediately |

Setup failures are reported in the exploration output with the skip reason.

### 6. Timeout Configuration

| Level | Default | Env var override |
|-------|---------|-----------------|
| Session | 120s | `SHATTER_SETUP_TIMEOUT` (global ceiling) |
| File | 30s | `SHATTER_SETUP_TIMEOUT` |
| Function | 15s | `SHATTER_SETUP_TIMEOUT` |
| Execution | 5s | `SHATTER_SETUP_TIMEOUT` |

CLI: `--setup-timeout <seconds>` sets the global ceiling. Per-level defaults can be overridden in config (`session_setup.timeout`, `defaults.setup_timeout`, per-function `setup_timeout`).

### 7. CLI Flags

- `--setup-timeout <seconds>` — global setup/teardown timeout ceiling
- `--fail-on-error` — abort on any setup failure (default: skip dependents + continue)

## File Changes

### Phase 1: Protocol & Types

| File | Changes |
|------|---------|
| `shatter-core/src/config.rs` | Replace `SetupMode` with `SetupLevel`. Add `SessionSetupConfig`, `file_setup` field, `setup_timeout` fields, `discover_setup_files()`. Update `ResolvedFunctionConfig`. |
| `shatter-core/src/protocol.rs` | Replace `mode` with `level` in Setup/Teardown. Add `parent_context` to Setup. Change `setup_context` in Execute to `SetupContextStack`. Add `SetupContextStack` type. Rename `function` to `scope` in Setup/Teardown. |
| `shatter-core/src/test_arbitraries.rs` | Replace `arb_setup_mode()` with `arb_setup_level()`. Add `arb_setup_context_stack()`. |
| `shatter-ts/src/protocol.ts` | Replace `SetupMode` with `SetupLevel`. Update request/response interfaces. |
| `shatter-go/protocol/types.go` | Mirror protocol changes. |
| `shatter-rust/src/protocol.rs` | Mirror protocol changes. |

### Phase 2: SetupManager

| File | Changes |
|------|---------|
| `shatter-core/src/setup_manager.rs` | **New** — all setup lifecycle, context tracking, failure tracking, timeout |
| `shatter-core/src/lib.rs` | Add `pub mod setup_manager;` |

### Phase 3: Explorer & Orchestrator Integration

| File | Changes |
|------|---------|
| `shatter-core/src/explorer.rs` | Remove `send_setup`/`send_teardown` functions and inline lifecycle. Replace `setup_file`/`setup_mode` in `ExploreConfig` with `SetupManager` reference. Use `context_stack()` for Execute commands. |
| `shatter-core/src/orchestrator.rs` | Add setup support to `ExploreConfig`. Use `SetupManager` for function/execution setup. Replace `setup_context: None` with `context_stack()`. |
| `shatter-core/src/scan_orchestrator.rs` | Wire session + file setup lifecycle. `SetupManager::setup_session` before scan, `setup_file` per file, teardowns in reverse. |

### Phase 4: CLI Wiring

| File | Changes |
|------|---------|
| `shatter-cli/src/main.rs` | Add `--setup-timeout` and `--fail-on-error` flags. Resolve setup files from config + convention. Set `SHATTER_SETUP_TIMEOUT` env var. Wire setup config into all explore/scan paths. |

### Phase 5: Frontend Implementations

**TypeScript (extend existing):**

| File | Changes |
|------|---------|
| `shatter-ts/src/handlers.ts` | Handle `level` field and `parent_context`. Separate context caches per level. Pass parent contexts to setup functions. |
| `shatter-ts/src/setup-loader.ts` | Update `runSetup()` to accept `SetupLevel` and `parentContext`. Setup function signature: `setup(scope, parentContext?)`. Support async setup/teardown. |
| `shatter-ts/src/executor.ts` | Read `SHATTER_SETUP_TIMEOUT` for setup-specific timeout. |

**Go (implement from stubs):**

| File | Changes |
|------|---------|
| `shatter-go/protocol/handler.go` | Replace stubs with real implementations. Load Go setup files as compiled plugins or subprocess. Cache contexts per level. |
| `shatter-go/setup/loader.go` | **New** — Go setup module loader. |

**Rust (implement from stubs):**

| File | Changes |
|------|---------|
| `shatter-rust/src/handler.rs` | Replace stubs. Rust setup files compiled and executed as subprocesses, returning JSON context on stdout. |
| `shatter-rust/src/setup.rs` | **New** — Rust setup execution logic. |

### Phase 6: Convention Discovery

| File | Changes |
|------|---------|
| `shatter-core/src/config.rs` | `discover_setup_files()` — filesystem check for convention patterns |

## Testing

1. **Protocol round-trips** — all new types in all 4 language implementations
2. **SetupManager unit tests** — lifecycle ordering, context merging, failure propagation, timeouts
3. **SetupManager proptest** — arbitrary setup/teardown sequences maintain valid state
4. **Config tests** — convention discovery, config merging with new fields, `SetupLevel` serialization
5. **E2E tests** — new cases in `e2e_concolic.rs` exercising session + file setup with TS frontend
6. **Parity tests** — explorer.rs and orchestrator.rs produce identical behavior with setup
7. **Walkthrough** — add setup/teardown demo to `demo/walkthrough.sh`
8. **Frontend tests** — handler tests for each level in TS, Go, Rust

## Verification

1. `cargo test` — all core tests pass (Quick tier)
2. `cargo clippy -- -D warnings` — clean (Standard tier)
3. `cd shatter-ts && npm test` — TS handler/setup tests pass
4. `cd shatter-go && go test ./...` — Go handler/setup tests pass
5. `cd shatter-rust && cargo test` — Rust handler/setup tests pass
6. `cargo test --test e2e_concolic` — E2E pipeline with setup still works
7. `bash demo/walkthrough.sh --auto --delay 0` — walkthrough passes

## Implementation Order

Phases 1→6 are sequential. Phase 5 (TS/Go/Rust frontends) can be parallelized via separate worktrees. Each phase must pass its test tier before proceeding.

# Multi-Level Setup/Teardown — Issue Breakdown

## Context

Users need in-process setup and teardown for tests executed by Shatter — initializing databases, authentication keys, external services, etc. The current system supports two levels (`per_function` and `per_execution`) but lacks session-level and file-level setup, convention-based discovery, proper error propagation, separate timeouts, and implementations in Go/Rust frontends. The orchestrator path (`orchestrator.rs`) also completely ignores setup, violating the parallel-path parity rule.

No backward compatibility constraints — we can redesign the protocol cleanly.

Design reference: see the full implementation plan in the user's message for protocol types, config schema, SetupManager design, error propagation rules, timeout defaults, CLI flags, convention patterns, and user-facing examples.

---

## Epic

**Title:** Multi-level setup/teardown
**Type:** epic
**Priority:** 1
**Labels:** core, protocol, setup
**Description:** Four-level setup/teardown lifecycle (session → file → function → execution) with convention-based discovery, per-level timeouts, error propagation, and implementations in all frontends. Replaces the current two-level SetupMode system.
**Gate:** `--waits-for-gate all-children`

---

## Issues

### 1. SetupLevel protocol types

**Type:** feature | **Priority:** 1 | **Labels:** core, protocol
**Parent:** epic
**Files:** `shatter-core/src/protocol.rs`, `shatter-core/src/test_arbitraries.rs`
**Description:**
Replace `SetupMode` with `SetupLevel` enum (session/file/function/execution). Add `SetupContextStack` type. Update Setup command: rename `function` → `scope`, replace `mode` → `level`, add `parent_context: Option<SetupContextStack>`. Update Teardown: rename `function` → `scope`, replace `mode` → `level`. Change Execute `setup_context` from `Option<Value>` to `Option<SetupContextStack>`. Update test_arbitraries: replace `arb_setup_mode()` with `arb_setup_level()`, add `arb_setup_context_stack()`. Include proptest roundtrips for all new types.
**Acceptance:** `cargo test -p shatter-core` passes, `cargo clippy -p shatter-core -- -D warnings` clean.
**Blocked by:** nothing

### 2. Config schema for multi-level setup

**Type:** feature | **Priority:** 1 | **Labels:** core, config
**Parent:** epic
**Files:** `shatter-core/src/config.rs`
**Description:**

# Multi-Level Setup/Teardown for Shatter

## Context

Users need in-process setup and teardown for tests executed by Shatter — initializing databases, authentication keys, external services, etc. The current system supports two levels (`per_function` and `per_execution`) but lacks session-level and file-level setup, convention-based discovery, proper error propagation, separate timeouts, and implementations in Go/Rust frontends. The orchestrator path (`orchestrator.rs`) also completely ignores setup, violating the parallel-path parity rule.

No backward compatibility constraints — we can redesign the protocol cleanly.

## User-Facing Examples

### TypeScript Setup Files

**Session-level setup** (`shatter.setup.ts` in project root, or `.shatter/setup.ts`):

```typescript
// shatter.setup.ts — runs once for the entire shatter session

import { Pool } from "pg";

let pool: Pool;

export async function setup(): Promise<{ db: Pool }> {
  pool = new Pool({ connectionString: process.env.DATABASE_URL });
  await pool.query("CREATE SCHEMA IF NOT EXISTS shatter_test");
  return { db: pool };
}

export async function teardown(context: { db: Pool }): Promise<void> {
  await context.db.query("DROP SCHEMA IF EXISTS shatter_test CASCADE");
  await context.db.end();
}
```

**File-level setup** (`src/db/users.shatter.setup.ts` co-located, or configured via glob):

```typescript
// users.shatter.setup.ts — runs once per source file being explored

export async function setup(
  scope: string,             // the source file path being explored
  parentContext: { db: Pool } // context from session setup
): Promise<{ table: string }> {
  const table = `test_users_${Date.now()}`;
  await parentContext.db.query(`CREATE TABLE ${table} (id SERIAL, name TEXT)`);
  return { table };
}

export async function teardown(
  scope: string,
  context: { table: string },
  parentContext: { db: Pool }
): Promise<void> {
  await parentContext.db.query(`DROP TABLE IF EXISTS ${context.table}`);
}
```

**Function-level setup** (configured in `.shatter/config.yaml`):

```typescript
// .shatter/setup/auth-setup.ts — runs once per function

export async function setup(
  scope: string,                              // function name
  parentContext: { db: Pool; table: string }   // merged session + file contexts
): Promise<{ authToken: string }> {
  const token = await generateTestToken(parentContext.db);
  return { authToken: token };
}

export async function teardown(
  scope: string,
  context: { authToken: string },
  parentContext: { db: Pool; table: string }
): Promise<void> {
  await revokeToken(context.authToken, parentContext.db);
}
```

**Execution-level setup** (runs before every single execution of a function):

```typescript
// .shatter/setup/per-exec-setup.ts — runs before each execution

export function setup(
  scope: string,
  parentContext: { db: Pool; table: string; authToken: string }
): { requestId: string } {
  return { requestId: crypto.randomUUID() };
}

// teardown is optional — if omitted, no teardown runs at this level
```

### Go Setup Files

**Session-level setup** (`shatter_setup.go` in project root):

```go
package shatter_setup

import (
    "database/sql"
    _ "github.com/lib/pq"
)

func Setup() (map[string]any, error) {
    db, err := sql.Open("postgres", os.Getenv("DATABASE_URL"))
    if err != nil {
        return nil, err
    }
    return map[string]any{"db_dsn": os.Getenv("DATABASE_URL")}, nil
}

func Teardown(context map[string]any) error {
    // cleanup
    return nil
}
```

### Rust Setup Files

**Session-level setup** (`shatter_setup.rs` in project root):

```rust
// Compiled and executed as a subprocess that communicates via JSON on stdout

use serde_json::json;

fn main() {
    // Setup logic
    let context = json!({"db_url": std::env::var("DATABASE_URL").unwrap()});
    println!("{}", context); // return context as JSON
}
```

### Configuration

**Convention-based** (zero config — just place files):

```
my-project/
├── shatter.setup.ts              # session setup (auto-discovered)
├── src/
│   ├── db/
│   │   ├── users.ts              # source file under test
│   │   └── users.shatter.setup.ts # file-level setup (auto-discovered)
│   └── auth/
│       ├── login.ts
│       └── login.shatter.setup.ts
└── .shatter/
    └── config.yaml               # optional overrides
```

**Config-based** (`.shatter/config.yaml`):

```yaml
# Session-level setup — runs once for the entire shatter run
session_setup:
  file: ./setup/global-init.ts
  timeout: 60   # seconds

# File-level setup — glob patterns map source files to setup files
file_setup:
  "src/db/**":   ./setup/db-file-init.ts
  "src/auth/**": ./setup/auth-file-init.ts

# Function-level defaults
defaults:
  setup: ./setup/default-function-setup.ts
  setup_level: function     # function | execution
  setup_timeout: 30

# Per-function overrides
functions:
  "src/auth.ts:validateToken":
    setup: ./setup/auth-token-setup.ts
    setup_level: execution   # run before every execution
    setup_timeout: 10
```

**CLI usage:**

```bash
# Basic — convention discovery handles everything
shatter explore src/db/users.ts

# With explicit setup timeout
shatter explore src/db/users.ts --setup-timeout 60

# Abort on any setup failure
shatter explore src/db/users.ts --fail-on-error

# Scan with setup (session setup runs once, file setups per file)
shatter scan src/ --fail-on-error --setup-timeout 120
```

## Setup Levels

| Level | Scope | Setup function signature |
|-------|-------|------------------------|
| **Session** | Once for entire shatter run | `setup() → context` |
| **File** | Once per source file | `setup(filePath, sessionContext) → context` |
| **Function** | Once per function | `setup(functionName, {session, file}) → context` |
| **Execution** | Per individual execution | `setup(functionName, {session, file, function}) → context` |

Each level's `setup()` receives the accumulated parent contexts. Each level's `teardown()` receives its own context plus the parent contexts.

## Design

### 1. Protocol

Replace `SetupMode` with `SetupLevel` everywhere. Remove backward-compat shims.

**`SetupLevel` enum:**

```rust
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, Deserialize, Serialize)]
#[serde(rename_all = "snake_case")]
pub enum SetupLevel {
    Session,
    File,
    Function,
    Execution,
}
```

**`Setup` command:**

```rust
Setup {
    file: String,           // path to the setup file
    scope: String,          // "" for session, file path for file, fn name for function/execution
    level: SetupLevel,
    parent_context: Option<SetupContextStack>,  // accumulated parent contexts
    project_root: Option<String>,
}
```

**`Teardown` command:**

```rust
Teardown {
    scope: String,
    level: SetupLevel,
}
```

**`SetupContextStack`** (replaces flat `setup_context`):

```rust
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Default, Serialize, Deserialize)]
pub struct SetupContextStack {
    pub session: Option<serde_json::Value>,
    pub file: Option<serde_json::Value>,
    pub function: Option<serde_json::Value>,
    pub execution: Option<serde_json::Value>,
}
```

**`Execute` command** — `setup_context: Option<Value>` → `setup_context: Option<SetupContextStack>`

**`Setup` response** — unchanged: `Setup { setup_context: Value }`

The core engine places each level's returned context into the appropriate `SetupContextStack` slot before sending Execute commands.

### 2. Config Schema

New fields in `ShatterConfig` (top level):
- `session_setup: Option<SetupConfig>` where `SetupConfig { file: String, timeout: Option<u64> }`
- `file_setup: Option<HashMap<String, String>>` (glob → setup file path)

Rename in `DefaultsConfig` and `FunctionConfig`:
- `setup_mode: Option<SetupMode>` → `setup_level: Option<SetupLevel>`
- Add `setup_timeout: Option<u64>`

`ResolvedFunctionConfig`:
- `setup_mode: SetupMode` → `setup_level: SetupLevel`
- Add `setup_timeout: u64`

### 3. Convention-Based Discovery

New function `discover_setup_files()` in `config.rs`:

| Level | Convention | Location |
|-------|-----------|----------|
| Session | `shatter.setup.{ext}` | Project root |
| Session | `.shatter/setup.{ext}` | Inside `.shatter/` dir |
| File | `<stem>.shatter.setup.{ext}` | Co-located with source file |

Where `{ext}` matches the frontend language (`ts`/`js` for TypeScript, `go` for Go, `rs` for Rust).

Config-based setup always takes precedence over convention-discovered files.

### 4. SetupManager (new module: `shatter-core/src/setup_manager.rs`)

Centralizes all setup lifecycle logic, replacing the inline logic in `explorer.rs`:

```rust
pub struct SetupManager {
    session_context: Option<Value>,
    file_contexts: HashMap<String, Value>,
    function_contexts: HashMap<String, Value>,
    failures: SetupFailures,
    fail_on_error: bool,
    timeouts: SetupTimeouts,
}

pub struct SetupTimeouts {
    pub session: Duration,
    pub file: Duration,
    pub function: Duration,
    pub execution: Duration,
}

pub struct SetupFailures {
    pub session: Option<String>,
    pub files: HashMap<String, String>,
    pub functions: HashMap<String, String>,
}
```

Key methods:
- `setup_session()` / `teardown_session()` — called by CLI/scan orchestrator
- `setup_file()` / `teardown_file()` — called when transitioning between source files
- `setup_function()` / `teardown_function()` — called by explorer/orchestrator per function
- `setup_execution()` / `teardown_execution()` — called per execution in the explore loop
- `context_stack(file, function)` → builds `SetupContextStack` from active contexts
- `should_skip_file(file)` / `should_skip_function(fn)` → checks failures

All setup calls are wrapped with `tokio::time::timeout()` using per-level timeouts.

### 5. Error Propagation

| Failure level | Effect | `--fail-on-error` |
|---------------|--------|-------------------|
| Session | All functions across all files skipped | Abort immediately |
| File | All functions in that file skipped | Abort immediately |
| Function | That function skipped | Abort immediately |
| Execution | Recorded as error, loop continues | Abort immediately |

Setup failures are reported in the exploration output with the skip reason.

### 6. Timeout Configuration

| Level | Default | Env var override |
|-------|---------|-----------------|
| Session | 120s | `SHATTER_SETUP_TIMEOUT` (global ceiling) |
| File | 30s | `SHATTER_SETUP_TIMEOUT` |
| Function | 15s | `SHATTER_SETUP_TIMEOUT` |
| Execution | 5s | `SHATTER_SETUP_TIMEOUT` |

CLI: `--setup-timeout <seconds>` sets the global ceiling. Per-level defaults can be overridden in config (`session_setup.timeout`, `defaults.setup_timeout`, per-function `setup_timeout`).

### 7. CLI Flags

- `--setup-timeout <seconds>` — global setup/teardown timeout ceiling
- `--fail-on-error` — abort on any setup failure (default: skip dependents + continue)

# Multi-Level Setup/Teardown — Issue Breakdown

## Context

Users need in-process setup and teardown for tests executed by Shatter — initializing databases, authentication keys, external services, etc. The current system supports two levels (`per_function` and `per_execution`) but lacks session-level and file-level setup, convention-based discovery, proper error propagation, separate timeouts, and implementations in Go/Rust frontends. The orchestrator path (`orchestrator.rs`) also completely ignores setup, violating the parallel-path parity rule.

No backward compatibility constraints — we can redesign the protocol cleanly.

Design reference: see the full implementation plan in the user's message for protocol types, config schema, SetupManager design, error propagation rules, timeout defaults, CLI flags, convention patterns, and user-facing examples.
